summary: DHH argues that being wrong is a valuable experience because it leads to learning and improved judgment. He frames decision-making as probabilistic — anyone making enough calls will inevitably be wrong some percentage of the time. He contends that a perfect track record simply means you never took risks. The post encourages swinging confidently while maintaining the humility to learn from mistakes.
key_points: Being wrong is a feature, not a bug — it corrects misconceptions and builds humility
Decision-making should be thought of in bets: some percentage of calls will inevitably be wrong
A perfect record means you never took real swings — staying safe isn't the same as being right
The goal is to keep making bold judgment calls while embracing mistakes as learning opportunities
money_quotes: It's a treat to be wrong.

If you hold a hundred opinions or make a hundred judgement calls, you're guaranteed that some percentage of those opinions will be misinformed and some of those calls will turn out poorly. That's just the math of being human.
Even when you think you're right. Especially when you're convinced you're right.
Anyone with a perfect batting record has never swung at a fast ball.
It's easy being right all the time if you stay out of the game.
Always swing confidently to the best of your ability. Always embrace a bad hit with the humility of a learner.
Loving the wrong makes the right.
key_insight: Thinking in bets — accepting that a percentage of your confident judgments will be wrong — is not a reason to stop swinging but a reason to swing with humility.
